text: The surgeon performs robotic transeptominal intersphincteric resection, starting with a total mesorectal excision (TME) approach. Dissection proceeds toward the pelvic floor along the vascular plane between the mesorectum and presacral fascia. After completing lateral and dorsal dissections, the anterior plane is dissected, dividing the peritoneal reflection. The rectovaginal septum is dissected, and the vaginal wall is mobilized using monopolar cautery, separating it from the rectum. The mesorectum ends around one to two centimeters above the anorectal angle. TME is performed down to the anorectal junction and levator ani plane. Intersphincteric resection begins from the left side of the rectum between the rectal wall and puborectal muscle, with the puborectal muscle located medially to the pubococcygeal muscle. The intersphincteric dissection continues distally and circumferentially, completing the dissection of the intersphincteric space. The rectum is then divided using an endogia stapler.

The surgeon performs robotic transabdominal intersphincteric resection, starting with a total mesorectal excision (TME) approach. Dissection proceeds toward the pelvic floor along the vascular plane between the mesorectum and presacral fascia. After completing lateral and dorsal dissections, the anterior plane is dissected, dividing the peritoneal reflection and continuing through the rectovaginal septum. The vaginal wall is mobilized using monopolar cautery, separating it from the rectum. The mesorectum ends around one to two centimeters above the anorectal angle. TME is performed down to the anorectal junction and levator ani plane. Intersphincteric resection begins on the left side of the rectum between the rectal wall and puborectal muscle, with the puborectal muscle located medially to the pubococcygeal muscle. The resection removes partial or total internal anal sphincter muscles without injuring external sphincter muscles. The intersphincteric dissection is completed distally and circumferentially, and the rectum is divided using an endogia stapler.

The surgeon performs pelvic lymph node dissection, resecting lymph nodes and fatty tissues in the area of the common iliac vessels. The left common iliac vein is visible in front of the S-I and L-V vertebral bodies. An enlarged lymph node is observed in the precycal area. The presacral lymph nodes overlying the sacral promontory are also removed, with careful dissection to avoid injury to the iliac vein.

The surgeon performs pelvic lymph node dissection, resecting lymph nodes and fatty tissues in the common iliac vessel area. The left common iliac vein is visible, located in front of the S-I and L-V vertebral bodies. An enlarged lymph node is observed in the precycal area. The presacral lymph nodes overlying the sacral promontory are also removed, with careful dissection to avoid injury to the iliac vein.

The surgeon dissects the lateral lymph nodes around the external iliac artery, internal iliac artery, and obturator fossa. The left ureter is visible crossing in front of the bifurcation of the common iliac artery and is carefully mobilized to prevent injury. Enlarged lymph nodes are observed in the left internal iliac region. The dissection includes lymph nodes and fatty tissue within the obturator fossa, below the obturator nerve, which is identified medial to the external iliac artery and lateral to the superior vesicle artery. The left external iliac vein is seen traveling on the medial side of the left iliac artery.

The surgeon dissects the lateral lymph nodes around the external iliac artery, internal iliac artery, and obturator fossa. The left ureter is identified crossing in front of the bifurcation of the common iliac artery and is mobilized to prevent injury. Enlarged lymph nodes are visible around the left internal iliac region. The dissection includes lymph nodes and fatty tissue within the obturator fossa, below the obturator nerve, which is identified medial to the external iliac artery and lateral to the superior vesicle artery. The left external iliac vein is seen traveling on the medial side of the left iliac artery. The obturator lymph nodes are resected while preserving the obturator nerve.

The surgeon resects the obturator lymph nodes while preserving the obturator nerve. A stable coloanal anastomosis is performed under laparoscopic vision, with the post-operative endoscope confirming the anastomosis line near the dentate line.
